title: Lebkuchen, German Christmas spice biscuits
subtitle: Honey-and-spice biscuits with a sugar-glaze top
excerpt: German lebkuchen in the Nuremberg household tradition: honey, dark sugar, ground almonds, candied peel and a strong dose of spice. Rolled, cut into rounds, baked soft, and finished with a thin sugar glaze. Twenty-four biscuits.
sourceNotes: Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1845, public domain): documents the canonical German lebkuchen form. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg: similar honey-and-spice biscuits appear in the cakes chapter as 'gingerbread of Lubec'. The pre-1928 trade gives the working ratio (one part honey to one part flour, by weight) used here.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake and glaze; freeze in a tin layered with baking paper for up to 3 months. The flavour mellows over the first week of keeping.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough at least 12 hours ahead (the rest improves the spice flavour); a 3-day rest in the fridge is better still. Glazed biscuits keep in an airtight tin for 2 weeks; flavour improves over the first 3 days.

recipeTools.notes: For warming the honey and sugar.

glossaryTerms.definition: Lebkuchen dough is kept in the fridge for at least 12 hours, and often 3 days, before being rolled and cut. The rest lets the honey hydrate the flour evenly, blooms the spice flavours, and produces a more pliable dough that rolls and cuts cleanly. Skipping the rest gives a tough, dry biscuit with raw-tasting spice.

content.text: Lebkuchen are German Christmas biscuits with honey as the primary sweetener: the honey is warmed with brown sugar to a thin syrup, poured into a bowl of flour, ground almonds, mixed peel and a generous hand of spice, brought together, and then rested in the fridge for at least 12 hours. The rest is non-negotiable; without it the biscuits bake tough and the spices read raw.

The biscuit itself is soft rather than crisp — closer to a thick spiced cake than to a snap biscuit. A thin sugar-and-water glaze is brushed over the warm biscuits as they come from the oven; it dries to a fine matte shell.

Warm the {{honey}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}} and {{unsalted-butter}} together in a saucepan over a low heat until smooth and just thinned. Cool for 5 minutes. Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{almond-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{cloves}} and {{mixed-peel}} in a wide bowl. Pour in the warm honey mix and add the beaten {{eggs}}. Stir to a soft, sticky dough. Wrap and
rest in the fridge for at least 12 hours

Heat the oven to 160°C fan and line two baking trays. Roll the rested dough on a lightly floured surface to about 8 mm thick. Cut 24 biscuits with a 6 mm round cutter. Set on the trays with 3 cm between each. Bake for 13–15 minutes; the biscuits should puff slightly, the tops should look set and matte, and the colour should be a deep tan.

While the biscuits bake, whisk the {{icing-sugar}} with the {{water}} to a thin pourable glaze. As soon as the biscuits come out, brush the tops with the glaze; it dries to a fine matte shell as the biscuits cool. Store in an airtight tin; the flavour improves over the first 3 days.
